name: John Coltrane
description: Saxophonist and composer whose restless evolution — from hard bop through modal jazz to free improvisation — redefined the possibilities of the tenor and soprano saxophone.

name: Radiohead
description: Oxford five-piece whose arc from guitar-band anthems through electronic reinvention made them one of the most critically acclaimed acts of their generation.

name: Bonnie "Prince" Billy
description: Will Oldham's three-decade catalog — from the raw Palace Brothers recordings through the Bonnie 'Prince' Billy years — is one of American music's most singular and restless bodies of work.

name: Veronica Falls
description: London-Glasgow quartet whose jangly, reverb-drenched guitar pop threaded C86 sweetness with gothic undertones across two near-perfect albums before quietly disbanding.

name: Miles Davis
albums: 60+ total (27 essential studio + 7 key live highlighted)
description: The most restlessly innovative figure in jazz history, whose unbroken arc of reinvention — from bebop through cool jazz, modal jazz, post-bop, and fusion — redefined the music at every turn.

name: Pixies
description: The loud-quiet-loud architects of alternative rock, whose taut surrealist songs — fusing punk velocity with pop melody and surf guitar — detonated a blueprint that shaped the sound of the 1990s.

name: PJ Harvey
description: One of the most fearless and shape-shifting artists in rock, whose three-decade career — from raw blues-punk fury through gothic Americana, spectral piano ballads, and politically charged art-rock — is defined by constant reinvention.

name: Destroyer
description: Dan Bejar's shape-shifting vehicle for three decades of literate, self-referential art-rock — moving restlessly from lo-fi home recordings through glam, MIDI orchestration, soft-rock sophistication, and electronic abstraction — while maintaining one of indie music's most distinctive lyrical voices.

name: The Strokes
description: Five New Yorkers whose taut, wired debut single-handedly reignited guitar rock at the turn of the millennium — fusing Velvet Underground cool, Television's guitar geometry, and a downtown swagger into a sound that launched a thousand bands.

name: Sonic Youth
description: New York's great noise architects, whose three decades of alternate tunings, feedback sculpting, and downtown art-world ambition — from no-wave abrasion through SST-era guitar epics to a decade on a major label — redrew the boundaries of what a rock band could sound like and mean.

name: Wu-Tang Clan
description: Staten Island's nine-man army whose RZA-orchestrated debut fused gritty street narratives with kung-fu cinema and dusty soul loops, launching a dynasty of solo careers and redefining hip-hop as a collective enterprise.

name: GZA
description: Wu-Tang's cerebral sword — whose dense, chess-metaphor lyricism and RZA's icy production on Liquid Swords created one of the most acclaimed albums in hip-hop history.

name: Ghostface Killah
description: Wu-Tang's most prolific and vivid storyteller, whose stream-of-consciousness narratives — woven through soul samples, luxury-brand namedropping, and cinematic street tales from Ironman through Supreme Clientele and beyond — produced the Clan's deepest solo catalog.

name: Method Man
description: Wu-Tang's most charismatic voice — whose gravel-toned baritone, effortless punchlines, and chemistry with Redman made him the Clan's crossover star across music, film, and television.

name: Raekwon
description: The Chef — whose Only Built 4 Cuban Linx... invented mafioso rap as a cinematic subgenre, and whose two-decade solo run delivered some of Wu-Tang's most vivid and ruthlessly detailed street narratives.

name: RZA
description: Wu-Tang's mastermind producer and philosopher-king, whose Bobby Digital alter ego and genre-defying solo work extended his vision beyond the Clan — from grimy boom-bap through cinematic scoring and digital-age experimentation.

name: Ol' Dirty Bastard
description: Wu-Tang's unhinged id — whose raw, unfiltered vocal style careened between singing, screaming, and stream-of-consciousness rapping with a chaotic energy that no one before or since has replicated.

name: Inspectah Deck
description: Wu-Tang's most underrated pen — whose scene-stealing verses on Clan records gave way to a steady solo career and a prolific second act as one-third of CZARFACE, channeling comic-book aesthetics into hard-hitting boom-bap.

name: U-God
description: Wu-Tang's golden-armed warrior, whose gruff baritone and rugged delivery across five solo albums carved out a workmanlike catalog in the shadow of the Clan's more celebrated soloists.

name: Masta Killa
description: The last Wu-Tang member to debut solo — whose patient, understated flow and loyalty to the Clan's original sound produced a quiet, consistent catalog anchored by the RZA-blessed No Said Date.

name: Cappadonna
description: Wu-Tang's unofficial tenth member, whose scene-stealing verse on 'Winter Warz' and prominent role on Wu-Tang Forever earned him a prolific if uneven solo career — the Clan's most relentless independent grinder.
